instructions_en: 1) Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, next to a large tire or similar heavy object that you can drag. Position yourself so that you are facing the tire, with both hands gripping the top edge.
2) Engage your core muscles and maintain a straight back throughout the exercise to stabilise your torso.
3) Lean forward slightly and begin to pull the tire towards you by extending your arms and driving through your legs.
4) Keep a steady pace as you drag the tire across the ground, using short, controlled steps to maintain balance and momentum.
5) Continue pulling the tire until it reaches your feet or as far as your space allows, maintaining proper form throughout.
6) To return the tire to its starting position, carefully reverse the pulling motion, maintaining control and stability.
7) Repeat the dragging motion for the desired number of repetitions or distance, focusing on maintaining a strong core and stable body position throughout the exercise.
The tire drag exercise targets your upper body, core, and legs, helping to improve strength, endurance, and overall fitness.
1) Stand beside a low pulley cable machine with the cable attached to your ankle. Keep your core engaged and hold onto the machine for balance. Allow the leg to cross over in front of the other.
2) Lift your leg sideways against the resistance of the cable, keeping it straight.
3) Pause briefly at the top of the movement squeezing the glutes and outer thigh, then slowly lower your leg back down.
4) Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch sides.
5) Focus on controlled movements and feel the tension in your outer hip muscles.
This exercise targets hip abduction and helps strengthen the muscles around your hips.
1) Stand in front of the SkiErg machine with your feet shoulder-width apart. Grip the handles firmly with an overhand grip, arms extended overhead. This will be your starting position.
2) Engage your core muscles and maintain a straight back as you prepare to initiate the movement. Ensure that your knees are slightly bent and your weight is evenly distributed.

3) Initiate the movement by pulling the handles down in a controlled motion. Begin by bending your knees slightly and hinging at your hips, driving your hips backward while maintaining a straight back.
4) As you pull the handles down, engage your lats and arms to bring them towards your thighs. Your hands should travel along the sides of your body in a smooth, fluid motion.
5) At the bottom of the movement, bend your knees further and squat down slightly, allowing your arms to extend fully behind your hips. This position mimics a powerful skiing motion.
6) Reverse the movement by extending your knees and hips to stand back up, simultaneously guiding the handles back to the starting position with your arms extended overhead.
7) Repeat the pulling motion for the desired time, calories, metres, maintaining a steady and controlled rhythm.
8) Focus on engaging your core and legs throughout the exercise. Avoid using only your arms; instead, generate power through your entire body.
The SkiErg exercise is an excellent full-body workout that builds cardiovascular endurance and strengthens your upper and lower body.

1) Position yourself on a sturdy bench or chair, facing away from it, and place your hands shoulder-width apart on the edge of the bench, fingers pointing forward. Have your knees bent and place a weight plate on your thighs. This is your starting position.
2) Lower your body by bending your elbows, allowing them to flare out to the sides, until your upper arms are parallel to the ground or your elbows are at approximately 90-degree angles.
Pause briefly at the bottom of the movement, feeling the stretch in your chest and triceps.
3) Press through the palms of your hands and extend your elbows to return to the starting position, straightening your arms fully without locking out your elbows.
4) Repeat the movement for the desired number of repetitions, focusing on controlled movements and maintaining stability in your core.
Weighted bench dips effectively target your triceps, chest, and shoulders, helping to build strength and muscle mass in the upper body.

name_en: Single Arm Front Incline Dumbbell Raise
instructions_en: 1) Sit on an incline bench set to a 45-degree angle, with your feet flat on the floor for stability. Hold a dumbbell in one hand, allowing it to hang straight down in front of you, while your other hand rests on your thigh or grips the bench for support. This will be your starting position.
2) Engage your core muscles to maintain stability in your torso. Ensure that your back is straight and your head is in line with your spine.
3) In a controlled motion, lift the dumbbell straight in front of you until your arm is parallel to the ground. Keep your elbow slightly bent to avoid locking the joint.
4) Pause for a moment at the top of the movement, feeling the contraction in your shoulder and upper chest muscles.
5) Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the starting position, maintaining control and avoiding any swinging or jerky movements.
6) Repeat the lift for the recommended number of repetitions, focusing on a smooth and controlled motion throughout.
7) Switch to the opposite arm and repeat the exercise, ensuring equal repetitions on both sides.
This exercise targets the anterior deltoid and helps improve shoulder strength and stability.
1)Sit on a bench at an incline, holding a dumbbell in each hand.
2) Keeping a slight bend in your elbows, raise one dumbbell in front of you to shoulder height.
3) Lower it back down, then repeat with the opposite arm.
4) Continue alternating arms for the desired number of repetitions.
5) Focus on controlled movements and keep your core engaged.
This exercise targets your shoulders and helps improve shoulder strength and stability.

name_en: Chest Supported Single Arm Dumbbell Front Raises
instructions_en: 1) Position yourself face down on an incline bench, chest supported, with a dumbbell in one hand.
2) Let your arm hang straight down towards the floor, knuckles facing upwards.
3) Raise the dumbbell directly in front of you to shoulder height, keeping your arm straight with a slight bend.
4) Lower the dumbbell back down slowly and repeat.
5) Switch arms and repeat the movement.
6) Focus on controlling the motion and engaging your shoulder muscles throughout.

name_en: Chest Supported Single Arm Dumbbell Rear Lateral Raise
instructions_en: 1) Lie facedown on an incline bench, holding a dumbbell in one hand.
2) Keep your arm slightly bent and palm facing inward.
3) Lift the dumbbell out to the side until your arm is parallel to the ground.
4) Lower back down with control.
5) Switch sides and repeat.
6) Focus on engaging your shoulder muscles and maintaining stability throughout.
1) Lie on your back with your feet placed on the dome of a BOSU ball.
2) Press through your heels to lift your hips towards the ceiling, squeezing your glutes at the top.
3) Lower your hips back down with control whilst bracing the abs.
4) Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, focusing on engaging your glutes throughout the movement.

name_en: BOSU - Single Leg Glute Bridges (Foot On Dome)
instructions_en: 1) Position a BOSU ball with the dome side up.
2) Lie on your back and place one foot on the dome, keeping the other leg extended.
3) Press through your heel on the BOSU to lift your hips, forming a straight line from shoulders to knees.
4) Pause briefly at the top, then lower your hips back down.
5) Repeat for the required repetitions.
6) Switch legs and repeat.
7) Focus on stability and engage your glutes throughout.
This exercise targets your glutes and hamstrings.

name_en: BOSU - Single Leg Glute Bridges (Shoulders On Dome)
instructions_en: 1) Lie on your back with your shoulders on the dome of a BOSU ball and one foot flat on the ground, knee bent.
2) Lift your other leg straight up, keeping it in line with your torso.
3) Push through your grounded foot to lift your hips towards the ceiling, squeezing your glutes at the top.
4) Pause briefly, then lower your hips back down.
5) Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch legs.
6) Focus on stability and engaging your glutes throughout the movement.
1) Lie on your back with your shoulders resting on the dome of a BOSU ball and your feet flat on the ground, knees bent.
2) Push through your heels to lift your hips towards the ceiling, squeezing your glutes at the top.
3) Pause briefly, then lower your hips back down.
4) Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, focusing on maintaining stability and control.
This exercise targets your glutes and hamstrings while also engaging your core for balance.
1) Place a BOSU ball flat-side down on the floor. Lie on your back with your lower back centred on the dome.
2) Bend your knees and place your feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart. Cross your arms over your chest or place your hands lightly behind your head.
3) Engage your core muscles and lift your shoulder blades off the BOSU, curling your upper body towards your knees.
4) Think about closing the gab between your ribcage and hips.
5) Pause briefly at the top, then lower back down with control.
6) Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, focusing on controlled movements and maintaining tension in your core throughout.
1) Sit on a BOSU ball with your feet off the ground and knees bent. Lean back slightly to engage your core.
2) Extend your legs straight out while simultaneously leaning back further, forming a V-shape with your body.
3) Return to the starting position by bending your knees and bringing them towards your chest.
4) Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, focusing on controlled movements and engaging your core throughout.
1) Sit on the rounded side of a BOSU ball with your hands planted on the ground behind you for support.
2) Lean back slightly, engaging your core, and lift your legs off the ground, forming a V shape with your body.
3) Slowly lower your upper body towards the ground while simultaneously extending your legs out in front of you.
4) Pause briefly at the bottom of the movement, then return to the starting position by sitting up and bringing your knees towards your chest.
5) Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, focusing on controlled movements and maintaining balance on the BOSU ball.

name_en: BOSU - Side Plank With Rotation (Feet On Dome)
instructions_en: 1) Begin in a side plank position with your feet on the dome of a BOSU ball.
2) Keep hips stacked on top of each other and elbow directly under the shoulder.
3) Engage your core and lift your hips, forming a straight line from head to heels.
4) Rotate your torso, reaching your top arm underneath your body and towards the floor.
5) Return to the starting position and repeat the required repetitions on the same side.
6) Then repeat on the other side.
7) Focus on stability and controlled movements.
This exercise targets your obliques and improves core strength.
1) Start in a side plank position with your feet on the dome of the BOSU ball and your body forming a straight line from head to heels.
2) Elbow should be directly under shoulders and hips stacked on top of each other.
3) Hold this position, engaging your core and stabilising muscles.
4) Focus on maintaining proper alignment and balance.
5) Hold for the desired duration, then switch to the other side.
This exercise strengthens your core and improves balance and stability.

name_en: BOSU - Side Plank With Rotation (Forearm On Dome)
instructions_en: 1) Begin in a side plank position with your forearm on the dome of the BOSU ball and feet stacked.
2) Raise your top arm towards the ceiling, opening up your chest and rotating your torso then rotate and reach the arm under the body.
3) Repeat for the required repetitions.
4) Switch sides and repeat.
5) Focus on stability and controlled movements.
This exercise targets your obliques and improves core strength.
1) Start by placing a BOSU ball on the ground with the dome side up. Position yourself on your side with your forearm resting on the dome and your feet stacked or staggered.
2) Lift your hips off the ground, creating a straight line from your head to your heels.
3) Ensure hips are stacked on top of each other and elbow is directly under the shoulder.
4) Engage your core muscles and hold this position for the desired amount of time.
5) Switch sides and repeat.
6) Focus on stability and alignment throughout the exercise.
This variation of the side plank targets your core and improves balance.
1) Start in a plank position with your hands on the flat side of a BOSU ball.
2) Engage your core and keep your body in a straight line from head to heels.
3) Drive one knee towards your chest, then quickly switch legs, as if running in place.
4) Continue alternating legs at a moderate pace.
5) Focus on maintaining stability on the BOSU ball and keeping your core engaged throughout.
1) Start in a high plank position with your hands on the BOSU ball and your body forming a straight line from head to heels.
2) Drive one knee towards the opposite elbow, crossing your body under your torso.
3) Keep abs braced and hips as stable as possible.
4) Return to the starting position and repeat on the other side.
5) Continue alternating sides in a smooth, controlled motion.
6) Focus on engaging your core and maintaining stability on the BOSU ball throughout the exercise.
1) Place a BOSU ball on the ground with the flat side facing up.
2) Get into a plank position with your hands on the BOSU ball and your body in a straight line from head to heels.
3) Drive one knee towards the outside of the same elbow, then return to plank position.
4) Alternate legs, moving in a smooth, controlled motion.
5) Focus on engaging your core and maintaining stability throughout the exercise.
This variation of mountain climbers targets your core, shoulders, and hip flexors.
1) Stand with one foot on the center of a BOSU ball, flat side up and the other foot in front of you on the ground.
2) Lower your body by bending your knees until your back knee is just above the ground.
3) Keep your front knee aligned with your ankle and your torso upright.
4) Push through your front heel to return to the starting position.
5) Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch legs.
6) Focus on stability and balance throughout the movement.
1) Place a BOSU ball with the dome side facing up.
2) Place the ball of one foot on the center of the dome behind you.
3) Step forward with your other foot, maintaining balance and stability.
4) Lower your body into a split squat position by bending your front knee, keeping your back knee slightly bent.
5) Pause briefly at the bottom, then push through your front heel to return to the starting position.
6) Repeat for the desired number of repetitions on one leg before switching to the other.
7) Focus on stability and control throughout the movement.
This exercise targets your legs and glutes while also challenging your balance.
1) Stand with the flat side of the BOSU facing up in front of you.
2) Step forward onto the BOSU with one foot.
3) Lower your body by bending both knees until your front thigh is parallel to the ground.
4) Push through your front heel to return to the starting position.
5) Repeat for required repetitions on the same leg.
6) Complete on the other leg.
7) Focus on stability and balance.
This exercise targets your legs and core.
1) Stand with the dome side of the BOSU facing up in front of you.
2) Step forward onto the BOSU with one foot.
3) Lower your body by bending both knees until your front thigh is parallel to the ground.
4) Push through your front heel to return to the starting position.
5) Repeat for required repetitions on the same leg.
6) Complete on the other leg.
7) Focus on stability and balance.
This exercise targets your legs and core.
1) Stand on the flat side of a BOSU ball with one foot positioned in the center and the other foot extended behind you.
2) Lower your body into a lunge position, bending both knees until your front thigh is parallel to the ground.
3) Keep your back straight and core engaged throughout the movement.
4) Drive through the front heel to return to start position.
5) Repeat for required repetitions.
6) Repeat on the other side.
7) Focus on stability and balance while engaging your leg muscles.
1) Stand on the dome of a BOSU ball with one foot, keeping the other foot extended behind you.
2) Lower your body into a lunge position, bending your front knee and lowering your back knee towards the ground.
3) Hold this position for a few seconds, then return to the starting position.
4) Repeat the movement for required repetitions.
5) Repeat on the other side.
6)Focus on stability and balance throughout the exercise.
1) Stand on the flat side of the BOSU with your feet shoulder-width apart.
2) Engage your core and maintain balance as you lower into a squat, keeping your knees aligned with your toes.
3) Pause briefly at the bottom, then push through your heels to return to standing.
4) Repeat for desired reps, focusing on stability and control.
This exercise targets your legs and core.
1) Stand on the dome side of the BOSU ball with feet shoulder-width apart.
2) Engage your core and lower into a squat, keeping your knees aligned with your toes.
3) Pause briefly at the bottom, then push through your heels to return to the starting position.
4) Focus on stability and balance throughout the movement.
5) Repeat for desired repetitions.

name_en: BOSU - Side To Side Press Ups
instructions_en: To start this exercise kneel down on the floor, and lean forward so that you are now bearing weight on your arms. Have your hands placed where your chest would be on dome side. Now you should come up onto your feet so that your body is suspended above the floor, the only points of contact (with the floor) should be your feet and your hands on the BOSU. Your body should be in one straight line from head to toe and your arms perpendicular to the floor under your chest. This will be your starting position.
Place your right hand out to the right of the BOSU.

Pause for a brief moment, exhale and press through your palms to return your body along the same path to the starting position. Bring your right hand to the centre of the BOSU to meet the left and then place your left hand out to the left of the BOSU. Tip: Squeeze your pectoral muscles and triceps as you do so and when you get to the top of the movement, do not lock your elbows out.
Repeat the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Bench Press or Press Ups.

To start this exercise kneel down on the floor, and lean forward so that you are now bearing weight on your arms. Have your hands placed on each side of the BOSU (flat side). Now you should come up onto your feet so that your body is suspended above the floor, the only points of contact (with the floor) should be your feet and your hands on the BOSU. Your body should be in one straight line from head to toe and your arms perpendicular to the floor under your chest. This will be your starting position.
Whilst inhaling, slowly lower your chest towards the ground whilst keeping your elbows by your side.
Exhale and explosively press hard enough through your palms to return your body not only along the same path but to enable you to lift the BOSU off the floor.
When your body weight is on its descent, land into the next repetition.
Repeat the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Bench Press or Press Ups.

name_en: BOSU - Press Ups (Feet On Flat Side)
instructions_en: To start this exercise kneel down on the floor, and lean forward so that you are now bearing weight on your arms. Have your hands placed just outside of where your chest would be. Now lift both feet up onto the BOSU (flat side) so that your body is suspended above the floor, the only points of contact (with the floor) should be your hands and your feet on the BOSU (flat side). Your body should be in one straight line from head to toe and your arms perpendicular to the floor under your chest. This will be your starting position.
Whilst inhaling, slowly lower your chest towards the ground whilst keeping your elbows by your side.

Pause for a brief moment, exhale and press through your palms to return your body along the same path to the starting position. Tip: Squeeze your pectoral muscles and triceps as you do so and when you get to the top of the movement, do not lock your elbows out.
Repeat the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Bench Press or Dumbbell Chest Press.

To start this exercise kneel down on the floor, and lean forward so that you are now bearing weight on your arms. Have your hands placed just outside of where your chest would be. Now lift both feet up onto the BOSU (dome side) so that your body is suspended above the floor, the only points of contact (with the floor) should be your hands and your feet on the BOSU (dome side). Your body should be in one straight line from head to toe and your arms perpendicular to the floor under your chest. This will be your starting position.
Whilst inhaling, slowly lower your chest towards the ground whilst keeping your elbows by your side.

Pause for a brief movement, exhale and press through your palms to return your body along the same path to the starting position. Tip: Squeeze your pectoral muscles and triceps as you do so and when you get to the top of the movement, do not lock your elbows out.
Repeat the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Plank.

name_en: BOSU - Press Ups (Hands On Flat Side)
instructions_en: To start this exercise kneel down on the floor, and lean forward so that you are now bearing weight on your arms. Have your hands placed on each side of the BOSU (flat side). Now you should come up onto your feet so that your body is suspended above the floor, the only points of contact (with the floor) should be your feet and your hands on the BOSU. Your body should be in one straight line from head to toe and your arms perpendicular to the floor under your chest. This will be your starting position.
Whilst inhaling, slowly lower your chest towards the BOSU whilst keeping your elbows by your side.

Pause for a brief movement, exhale and press through your palms to return your body along the same path to the starting position. Tip: Squeeze your pectoral muscles and triceps as you do so and when you get to the top of the movement, do not lock your elbows out.
Repeat the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Bench Press or Press Ups.

To start this exercise kneel down on the floor, and lean forward so that you are now bearing weight on your arms. Have your hands placed where your chest would be on dome side. Now you should come up onto your feet so that your body is suspended above the floor, the only points of contact (with the floor) should be your feet and your hands on the BOSU. Your body should be in one straight line from head to toe and your arms perpendicular to the floor under your chest. This will be your starting position.
Whilst inhaling, slowly lower your chest towards the BOSU whilst keeping your elbows by your side.

Pause for a brief movement, exhale and press through your palms to return your body along the same path to the starting position. Tip: Squeeze your pectoral muscles and triceps as you do so and when you get to the top of the movement, do not lock your elbows out.
Repeat the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Bench Press or Press Ups.
1) Begin in a plank position with your feet on the dome side of the BOSU ball and your forearms on the ground.
2) Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels, engaging your core muscles.
3) Keep shoulders over elbows and hips down.
4) Hold this position for the desired amount of time, focusing on stability and control.
5) Remember to breathe steadily throughout the exercise.
This plank variation targets your core muscles and improves balance and stability.

To start this exercise kneel down on the floor, and lean forward so that you are now bearing weight on your arms. Have your hands placed just outside of where your chest would be. Now lift both feet up onto the BOSU (dome side) so that your body is suspended above the floor, the only points of contact (with the floor) should be your hands and your feet on the BOSU (dome side). Your body should be in one straight line from head to toe and your arms perpendicular to the floor under your chest. This will be your starting position.

Keep your abdominal muscles tight throughout this exercise and keep your pelvis in line with the rest of your body to minimise risk of injury to your lower back (you can elevate your pelvis slightly to reduce lower back pain).
Hold this position for as long as you can or for the recommended amount of time.
Variations: Plank.

Firstly, adopt an 'all-fours' position above on the floor. From here you will need to bend your elbows to a 90 degree angle and place your upper body weight onto your forearms on top of the BOSU (dome side). After you have done that, take a step backwards with your feet and place your remaining bodyweight through your toes. At this point your body should be suspended (in a straight line) above the floor with just your forearms and toes as points of contact. This will be your starting position.

Keep your abdominal muscles tight throughout this exercise and keep your pelvis in line with the rest of your body to minimise risk of injury to your lower back (you can elevate your pelvis slightly to reduce lower back pain).
Hold this position for as long as you can or for the recommended amount of time.
Variations: Plank.

To start this exercise kneel down on the floor, and lean forward so that you are now bearing weight on your arms. Have your hands placed where your chest would be on dome side. Now you should come up onto your feet so that your body is suspended above the floor, the only points of contact (with the floor) should be your feet and your hands on the BOSU. Your body should be in one straight line from head to toe and your arms perpendicular to the floor under your chest. This will be your starting position.

Keep your abdominal muscles tight throughout this exercise and keep your pelvis in line with the rest of your body to minimise risk of injury to your lower back (you can elevate your pelvis slightly to reduce lower back pain).
Hold this position for as long as you can or for the recommended amount of time.
Variations: Plank.
1) Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in each hand at arms length.
2) Swing the kettlebells through the legs and explosively push through your legs and hips to drive the kettlebells overhead, simultaneously splitting your stance so one foot is forward and one back.
3) Lower the kettlebells back down to shoulder height and return to the starting position.
4) Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
5) Focus on explosive power and stability throughout the movement.
Step on to the treadmill and make sure before you press anything, you are in the middle of the band with the safety clip attached to your top.
Next, select the desired settings. If possible, enter your statistics (age, sex, weight ect.) so you get more precise readings.
Continue until you have completed the prescribed amount of exercise.

name_en: Battle Ropes In And Out Waves

instructions_en: 1) Stand facing a cable machine with a rope attachment set up at shoulder height.
2) Grab the ends of the rope with an overhand grip.
3) Step back slightly and position your feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent.
4) Pull the rope towards your face, keeping elbows up high and spreading it apart as you pull.
5)  Squeeze your shoulder blades together.
6) Pause briefly at the peak contraction, then slowly return to the starting position.
7) Keep your core engaged and maintain a tall posture throughout the movement.
8) Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
This exercise targets your rear deltoids and upper back muscles.
1) Stand facing a cable machine set up with a low pulley.
2) Grab onto the rope attachment and have feet just wider than shoulder width, toes slightly turned out.
3) Lower into a squat, keeping your back straight and chest up.
4) Pause briefly at the bottom, then push through your heels and squeeze the glutes to return to the starting position.
5) Focus on keeping tension on the cable throughout the movement.
6) Repeat for desired reps.
This exercise targets your legs and glutes while also engaging your core for stability.

name_en: Front Foot Elevated Split Squats Stretch
Cross Body Lat Stretch - Against A Wall

instructions_en: 1) Stand facing the TRX straps, holding the handles with your palms facing each other. Position your feet hip-width apart and lean back slightly to create tension in the straps. This will be your starting position.
2) Engage your core muscles to maintain stability and keep your back straight throughout the movement.
3) Shift your weight to one leg and lift the opposite leg off the ground, crossing it behind your standing leg.
4) Bend your knees and lower your hips into a squat position, allowing the rear leg to cross further behind you. Keep your front knee aligned with your toes and ensure your back remains straight.
5) Pause for a moment at the bottom of the movement, feeling the stretch in your glutes and thighs.
Push through your front heel to return to the starting position, bringing your rear leg back to the centre.
6) Repeat the movement on the opposite side, alternating between each leg for the recommended number of repetitions.
This exercise helps improve lower body strength, stability, and coordination.
1) Stand with feet shoulder-width apart over a landmine facing away from the anchor point.
2) Hold onto a V bar attachment with a bent over position.
3) Keep your back straight, abs braced and chest up.
4) Pull the V-bar towards your lower chest, squeezing your shoulder blades together and drawing your elbows towards your hips.
5) Slowly lower the V-bar back to the starting position.
6) Repeat for desired reps.
7) Focus on controlled movements and engage your back muscles throughout.
This exercise targets your upper back and biceps.

1) Position yourself under the Smith machine bar with your feet hip-width apart. The bar should be resting comfortably across your upper back and shoulders, with your hands gripping it slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.

2) Step back with one foot and lower your body into a reverse lunge, bending both knees to approximately 90-degree angles. Your front knee should be aligned with your ankle, and your back knee should hover slightly above the floor.
3) Maintain an upright torso and engage your core muscles for stability throughout the movement. Keep your chest lifted and your shoulders back.
4) Push through the heel of your front foot to return to the starting position, bringing your back foot forward to meet the other foot.
5) Repeat the movement on the same side for the desired reps before switching to the other leg.

This exercise targets your quadriceps, hamstrings, glutes, and calves, as well as engaging your core for stability. It's a great way to strengthen your lower body and improve balance and coordination.
1) Stand with one leg straight and the other against the foot pad of the glute machine with your knee bent.
2) Lean forward and grasp the handles or bars for stability.
3) Extend the leg straight back, squeezing your glutes at the top.
4) Pause briefly, then return to the starting position.
5) Repeat for the desired number of repetitions on each leg.
Focus on controlled movements and squeezing your glutes at the top of the movement.
1) Stand on the Hack Squat machine with your back against the pad and your feet nice and wide.
2) Place your feet higher on the platform with toes pointed slightly outward.
3) Grasp the handles and release the safety bars.
4) Lower your body by bending your knees until your thighs are parallel to the ground.
5) Push through your heels to return to the starting position.
6) Focus on maintaining proper form and engaging your leg muscles throughout the movement.
1) Sit on the lateral raise machine with your chest against the pad and feet flat on the ground.
2) Grasp the handles with a neutral grip and position your arms by your sides.
3) Exhale as you raise the handles out to the sides until your arms are parallel to the ground.
4) Pause briefly at the top, then inhale as you lower the handles back down to the starting position.
5) Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, focusing on controlled movements and maintaining proper form throughout.
This exercise targets the deltoid muscles in your shoulders.
1) Begin by standing on the treadmill with your feet shoulder-width apart and your body centered on the belt. Make sure the treadmill is set to a low speed to start.
2) Engage your core muscles to maintain stability as you start the treadmill and gradually increase the speed to a comfortable walking pace.
3) Once you've established a steady walking pace, increase the incline of the treadmill. Begin with a slight incline, and adjust as needed based on your fitness level and comfort.
4) As you walk, keep your posture upright with your shoulders relaxed and your arms swinging naturally by your sides. Avoid leaning too far forward or backward.
5) Focus on pushing through your heels with each step to engage your glutes and hamstrings. This will help to tone and strengthen your lower body muscles.
6) Continue walking on the inclined treadmill for the desired duration.
Incorporating incline walking into your treadmill routine can boost calorie burn, increase cardiovascular endurance, and target different muscle groups compared to walking on a flat surface.
1) Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell with both hands in front of you.
2) Hinge at your hips and slightly bend your knees while keeping your back straight.
3) Swing the kettlebell between your legs, then explosively thrust your hips forward and squeeze your glutes to swing it up to chest level.
4) Control the descent and repeat the movement for the desired number of repetitions.
5) Focus on using your hips to generate momentum and keep your core engaged throughout the exercise.
1) Stand facing a cable machine with a small bar attachment on a low pulley.
2) Grab the handle with an overhand grip, palms facing the ceiling in a clean positions and step back to create tension in the cable.
3) Keep the bar close to the chest and elbows lifted.
4) Position your feet shoulder-width apart with toes slightly turned out.
5) Lower into a squat, keeping your chest up and knees tracking over your toes.
6) Pause briefly at the bottom, then push through your heels and squeeze the glutes to return to the starting position.
7) Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
8) Focus on maintaining proper form and control throughout the exercise.
Cable front squats target your quads, glutes, and core muscles.
1) Stand with your feet hip-width apart, holding a barbell across your shoulders.
2) Step one foot diagonally behind and across the opposite leg, bending both knees into a curtsy position.
3) Push through your front heel to return to standing, then alternate sides.
4) Keep your chest up and core engaged throughout.
5) Repeat for desired repetitions. This exercise targets your glutes and legs.
1) Position a barbell across your upper back, resting it below your neck on your traps.
2) Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointed slightly outward.
3) Brace your core and squat down by bending your knees and hips, keeping your back straight.
4) Lower until your thighs are parallel to the ground, then push through your heels to return to the starting position.
5) Keep your chest up and back straight throughout the movement.
6) Repeat for desired reps, focusing on proper form and control.
1) Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding dumbbells in front of thighs.
2) Hinge at hips with a slight bend in the knees, keeping back straight, and lower dumbbells towards shins.
3) Pause when you feel a stretch in hamstrings, then drive through the heels and return to standing position squeezing the glutes.
4) Keep core engaged throughout.
This exercise targets hamstrings and lower back.

name_en: Chest Supported Alternating Dumbbell Front Raises
instructions_en: 1) Lie face down on an incline bench with a dumbbell in each hand, arms hanging straight down with an overhand grip.
2) Keeping your chest pressed against the bench, lift one dumbbell up to shoulder level while maintaining a slight bend in your elbow.
3) Lower the dumbbell back down and repeat on the opposite side.
4) Alternate between each arm for the desired number of repetitions.
5) Focus on controlled movements and engage your shoulder muscles throughout.
This exercise targets your front deltoids.
1) Place one arm against an incline bench with a dumbbell in hand, elbow resting on the bench and palm facing inward.
2) Keep your back straight and core engaged.
3) Curl the dumbbell towards your shoulder while keeping your upper arm stationary squeezing the bicep.
4) Pause briefly at the top, then lower the dumbbell back down.
5) Repeat for required repetitions and then swap arms.
6) Focus on controlled movements and avoid swinging the weight.
This exercise targets your biceps.

To start this exercise kneel down on the floor, and lean forward so that you are now weight baring on your arms. Have your hands placed just outside of where your chest would be. Now you should come up onto your feet so that your body is suspended above the floor, the only points of contact (with the floor) should be your hands and feet. Your body should be in one straight line from head to toe and your arms perpendicular to the floor under your chest. This will be your starting position.

Whilst inhaling, slowly lower your chest towards the ground whist flaring your elbows out wide. When you are about an inch or so from the ground, you should have created a triangle with your hand positions and your eye line.

Pause for a brief movement, exhale and press through your palms to return your body along the same path to the starting position. Tip: Squeeze your pectoral muscles as you do so and when you get to the top of the movement, do not lock your elbows out.
Repeat the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Bench Press or Dumbbell Chest Press.
Grab two dumbbells and take a seat on a bench which is just off vertical (70-80 degrees), and allow your arms to hang by your sides, perpendicular to the floor.
The palms of your hands should be facing each other. This will be your starting position. Tip: Throughout this movement your elbows should stay pinned to the sides of your torso.

Whilst holding the upper arm stationary, exhale as you curl both the dumbbells upward, contracting your biceps. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze your biceps. (Tip: Only the forearms should move.)
Inhale as you slowly begin to bring the dumbbells back along the same path, down to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Seated Hammer Curls or Cable Curls.
Grab two dumbbells and adopt a shoulder width, upright standing position with your back straight and arms by your side.
The palms of your hands should be facing your torso. This will be your starting position.

Whilst holding the upper arm stationary, exhale as you curl both dumbbells upward, contracting your biceps. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze your biceps. (Tip: Only the forearms should move.)
Inhale as you slowly begin to bring the dumbbells back along the same path, down to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Seated Hammer Curls or Cable Curls.
Grab two kettlebells and stand with a shoulder width stance, toes pointing slightly outward (palms facing the side of your legs).
Throughout the movement you should keep your head up and maintain a stright back. This will be your starting position.

Inhale and descend slowly by bending your knees until your thighs are parallel with the floor. Keep a straight back so that your posture is not compromised. Tip: When at the bottom of the movement your knees should be behind your toes.

Once you have hit the bottom of the movement, exhale and push hard through your heels to drive your hips forward. Extend at your knee and ankle joints in order to jump as high as you can before landing into your next rep.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: Always be aware of your posture with this exercise as if performed incorrectly, could cause injury.
Variations: Dumbbell Jump Squats or Barbell Jump Squats.
Grab two dumbbells and stand with a shoulder width stance, toes pointing slightly outward (palms facing the side of your legs).
Throughout the movement you should keep your head up and maintain a straight back. This will be your starting position.

Inhale and descend slowly by bending your knees until your thighs are parallel with the floor. Keep a straight back so that your posture is not compromised. Tip: When at the bottom of the movement your knees should be behind your toes.

Once you have hit the bottom of the movement, exhale and push hard through your heels to drive your hips forward. Extend at your knee and ankle joints in order to jump as high as you can before landing into your next rep.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: Always be aware of your posture with this exercise as if performed incorrectly, could cause injury.
Variations: Kettlebell Jump Squats or Barbell Jump Squats.

Grab a dumbbell and adopt a slightly wider than shoulder width stance. Bend at your knees and hips (more so with your hips than knees), maintaining a straight back and allowing your arm (with which you are holding the dumbbell) to hang perpendicular to the floor between your knees. Your chest, shoulders and head should be facing forward at this point. This will be your starting position.

Your first movement will be to drive through your heels and quads in order to start elevating the dumbbell, exhale as you do so. Your back should remain straight and your arm should still be perpendicular to the floor at this point.

Next comes the acceleration from your pelvis, you need to drive your pelvis forward when the dumbbell gets to the middle of your thighs. This coupled with the extension through your knee and ankle joints, will propel the dumbbell upward.
Once you have thrusted your hips forward, it is time to shrug as hard as you can in order to lift the dumbbell further. Flare your elbow up and outward until your body is at full extension.
When at full extension, aggressively squat under the dumbbell and rotate your elbow under and in so that you can take the load on your front deltoids (shoulders).
Drive upward until you are in a standing position, inhale at this point.

Once you are in a standing position, exhale and bend at your knees to generate power to press the dumbbell above your head until your elbow is locked out. Tip: Maintaining a straight back throughout is paramount.
Inhale and descend the dumbbell back into the starting position by reversing this movement, making sure to keep the dumbbell close to your body throughout and maintaining good posture.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap arms.
Variation: Barbell Clean & Press or Barbell Cleans.

Grab two dumbbells and take a neutral grip (palms facing each other). Curl the dumbbells up half way so your elbows are at 90 degrees, adopt a standing position with a shoulder width stance and a straight back. Keep your elbows pinned in close to your torso for the duration of the exercise. Tip: Only your forearms should move and your torso remain rigid. If you are struggling with balance, split your stance so you have one foot in front of the other. This will be your starting position.

This exercise is split up into 3 different elements. The bottom half, top half and a full range bicep curl. Firstly, using your biceps, lower the dumbbells down until your arms are straight (Inhale as you go). Once your arms are straight, use your biceps once more to curl the dumbbells back along the same path into their starting position, exhaling as you do so.

Once you have completed 7 repetitions, curl the dumbbells up (from half way) towards your shoulders. Exhale as you lift the dumbbells and continue until your biceps are squeezed tightly and you cannot elevate your forearms anymore without bringing your elbows away from your body. Once you have reached this point, return the dumbbells along the same path to their starting position whilst inhaling.

Once you have completed 7 repetitions, perform 7 full curls. Inhale as you lower the dumbbells towards your thighs so your arms are hanging perpendicular to the floor. Exhale and use your biceps to curl the dumbbells up towards your shoulders. Once your biceps are fully contracted, inhale and lower the dumbbells back along the same path into their starting position.
Variations: Cable 21's or EZ Bar 21's.

Grab a low cable with the bar attachment on and take an underhand grip (palms facing forward). Allow your arms the hang perpendicular to the floor and adopt a standing position with a shoulder width stance and a straight back. Keep your elbows pinned in close to your torso for the duration of the exercise. Tip: Only your forearms should move and your torso remain rigid. If you are struggling with balance, split your stance so you have one foot in front of the other. This will be your starting position.

This exercise is split up into 3 different elements. The bottom half, top half and a full range bicep curl. Firstly, whilst keeping your upper arms stationary, curl the bar upward towards your shoulders by flexing your biceps. Exhale as you do so but stop once you get half way. Return the bar along the same path back to the starting position as you inhale.

After 7 repetitions, curl the bar up to your shoulders and perform the top half of your curls. In order to do so, lower the bar whilst inhaling but stop once your elbows are at 90 degrees. Exhale and curl the bar back along the same path to the starting position (shoulders).

After 7 repetitions, Lower the bar all the way until your arms are straight. Once you are here, perform 7 full range repetitions. In order to do so, curl the bar up to your shoulders on an arced path, keeping your elbows pinned to your torso, exhaling as you go. Once you have paused for a brief moment and squeezed your biceps, return the bar along the same path back into the starting position.
Variations: Dumbbell 21's or EZ Bar 21's.

Grab an EZ curl bar with a narrow, underhand grip (palms facing forward). Allow your arms the hang perpendicular to the floor and adopt a standing position with a shoulder width stance and a straight back. Keep your elbows pinned in close to your torso for the duration of the exercise. Tip: Only your forearms should move and your torso remain rigid. If you are struggling with balance, split your stance so you have one foot in front of the other. This will be your starting position.

This exercise is split up into 3 different elements. The bottom half, top half and a full range bicep curl. Firstly, whilst keeping your upper arms stationary, curl the bar upward towards your shoulders by flexing your biceps (exhale as you do so). Once you are at your shoulders perform the top half of the movement. In order to do so, lower the bar whilst inhaling but stop once your elbows are at 90 degrees. Exhale and curl the bar back along the same path to the starting position (shoulders).

After 7 repetitions, Lower the bar down towards your thighs until your arms are straight (inhale as you do so). Now, exhale and curl the bar up towards your shoulders but stop once you get half way. Return the bar along the same path back to the starting position as you inhale.

After 7 repetitions, perform 7 full range movements. In order to do so, curl the bar up to your shoulders on an arced path, keeping your elbows pinned to your torso, exhaling as you go. Once you have paused for a brief moment and squeezed your biceps, return the bar along the same path back into the starting position.
Variations: Dumbbell 21's or Cable 21's.

Grab a barbell with a shoulder width, underhand grip (palms facing forward). Allow your arms the hang perpendicular to the floor and adopt a standing position with a shoulder width stance and a straight back. Keep your elbows pinned in close to your torso for the duration of the exercise. Tip: Only your forearms should move and your torso remain rigid. If you are struggling with balance, split your stance so you have one foot in front of the other. This will be your starting position.

This exercise is split up into 3 different elements. The bottom half, top half and a full range bicep curl. Firstly, whilst keeping your upper arms stationary, curl the bar upward towards your shoulders by flexing your biceps (exhale as you do so). Once you are at your shoulders perform the top half of the movement. In order to do so, lower the bar whilst inhaling but stop once your elbows are at 90 degrees. Exhale and curl the bar back along the same path to the starting position (shoulders).

After 7 repetitions, Lower the bar down towards your thighs until your arms are straight (inhale as you do so). Now, exhale and curl the bar up towards your shoulders but stop once you get half way. Return the bar along the same path back to the starting position as you inhale.

After 7 repetitions, perform 7 full range movements. In order to do so, curl the bar up to your shoulders on an arced path, keeping your elbows pinned to your torso, exhaling as you go. Once you have paused for a brief moment and squeezed your biceps, return the bar along the same path back into the starting position.
Variations: Dumbbell 21's or Cable 21's.

Grab a dumbbell in each hand and take a seat on a bench that is just of vertical (between 70-80 degrees). Bring the dumbbells up to the side of your shoulders so your elbows are flared out wide and at a 90 degree angle, your palms should be facing forward. This will be your starting position.
Whilst exhaling, press one of the dumbbells towards the ceiling until your elbow becomes fully extended (straight). Keep your other arm stationary in the starting position.
After a brief pause, return the dumbbell back along the same path to the starting position.
Repeat for the opposite side and alternate between arms.
Complete the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Standing Alternating Shoulder Press or Alternating Cable Shoulder Press.

Place a rope attachment on to a high cable pulley, adopt a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and stand 12 inches away from the machine. Once you have taken hold of the rope, bring your elbows in to the sides of your torso and keep them pinned in place. This is your starting position.
As you exhale, push the rope towards the floor and split your little fingers as much as possible until your elbows are fully extended.
At this position you should squeeze your triceps and pause for a second.
Then, as you inhale, reverse the movement until your elbows are flexed at 90 degrees and you have returned to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Tricep Press Down Machine or Dumbbell Tricep Kickbacks.

Grab a dumbbell in each hand and take a seat on a bench just off vertical (around 70-80 degrees). Press the dumbbells above your head so that you arms are fully extended, yet slightly flexed/bent at your elbows. Make sure your palms are facing forward at this point. This will be your starting position.

Slowly and in a controlled fashion, descend the dumbbells until they reach the side of your head (parallel with your ears), creating a 90 degree angle at your elbows. Inhale on the downward motion and flare your elbows out wide.
After a brief pause, exhale and press the dumbbells back along this path to their starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Standing Dumbbell Shoulder Press or Cable Shoulder Press.

Take a step forward (slightly larger than a normal step) and as you land, drop your body down so that you create two right angles at your knees. Inhale as you do so whilst maintaining a straight torso.
Make sure that you keep your front knee over your heel and not over your toes. Your front shin should now be perpendicular to the floor.

As you exhale, you need to direct force through your quadriceps and glutes in order to drive your body upward and into a jumping motion. As you leave the floor you will reverse your stance by bringing your back leg forward and your front leg back.
As you land, you land into the next repetition by bending at your knees and hips to create right angles at the joints of your front leg, inhale as you do so.
Alternate in this manner and repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Walking Lunges or Barbell Lunges.
Stand upright with your arms beside your torso and adopt a shoulder width stance. This will be your starting position.
Inhale and lift your left leg up infront of you until your knee and hip joints are at 90 degrees. Then have your right arm bent at a 90 degree position at your elbow.
Exhale and swap your left leg for your right and your right arm for your left.
Continue to elevate your knees in alternating fashion whilst swapping your arms over so they are always opposing your legs. Tip: Take shallow breaths whilst you are performing the movement.
Alternate as fast as you can to simulate a running motion but standing still. The higher you can get your knees, the better!
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variation: Ab Bike or Mountain Climbers.

Adopt a standing, shoulder width stance with your toes pointing slightly outward. Allow your arms to hang comfortably by your sides at this point. Throughout the movement you should keep your head up and maintain a straight back. This will be your starting position.

Inhale and descend slowly by bending your knees until your thighs are parallel with the floor. Keep a straight back so that your posture is not compromised. Bring your arms out in front of you so that they are also parallel to the floor at this point. Tip: When at the bottom of the movement your knees should be behind your toes.

Once you have hit the bottom of the movement, exhale and push hard through your heels to drive your hips forward. As you extend at your knee and ankle joints in order to jump as high as you can, pull your arms down towards the sides of your torso at the top of the jump (this will help to generate more height), before landing into your next rep.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: Always be aware of your posture with this exercise as if performed incorrectly, could cause injury.
Variations: Kettlebell Jump Squats or Dumbbell Jump Squats.
Set up a box/step/bench in front of you at the desired height. Stand upright with your arms beside your torso and adopt a shoulder width stance. This will be your starting position.

Inhale and descend into a squat position by bending at your knees and hips. Once your hips are at 90 degrees, exhale and press through your feet explosively using your thigh and glute muscles in order to generate enough power to jump up and onto the box/step/bench infront of you.
When you land on the platform, bend at your knee and hip joints once more and extend upward into a standing position.
Inhale and step back off the box/step/bench back into your starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Barbell Step Ups or Dumbbell Step Ups.
Stand upright with your arms beside your torso and adopt a shoulder width stance. This will be your starting position.

Inhale and descend into a press up position. Place your hands just wider than shoulder width and jump backward with your feet so that your legs are fully extended and your body is supported by your toes and hands.
Continue to drop your torso downward towards the floor by bending at your elbows until your chest is only a few inches away.

Once you get to this point, exhale and aggressively press through your hands by extending your elbow joints and create enough upward motion to be able to jump your legs back underneath your body so that when you take your hands off the floor, you should be in the bottom of a squat.

Now, press through the middle of your feet by using your thighs and glutes, extending at both your knee and hip joints to generate power for a jumping motion (you can use your arms at this point to emphasise lift).
As you land, inhale and go straight into the next repetition.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
1) Start by adjusting the TRX suspension mid-length and securing them firmly to a stable anchor point overhead.
2) Stand facing away the anchor point and grasp the handles of the TRX straps with both hands, palms facing down.
3) Position yourself in a plank position, keeping everything in a straight line. This is your starting position.
4) Engage your core muscles to maintain a straight line from your head to your heels throughout the exercise.
5) Bend your elbows and slowly lower your chest towards the ground, maintaining control and stability in your shoulders and core.
6) Pause briefly once you feel the stretch in your chest muscles.
7) Push through your palms and straighten your arms to return to the starting position, exhaling as you push up.
8) Repeat the movement for the desired number of repetitions, focusing on maintaining proper form and control throughout.
This exercise targets your chest, shoulders, and triceps while also engaging your core for stability.
1) Stand facing the TRX suspension trainer anchor point, holding the handles with your arms extended straight in front of you. This is your starting position.
2) Engage your core muscles to maintain stability and keep your chest lifted throughout the exercise.
3) Bend one knee and lift your foot off the ground.
4) Lower your body into a squat position on the standing leg, keeping your knee in line with your toes and your chest upright.
5) Pause briefly at the bottom of the squat, ensuring your knee does not collapse inward.
6) Push through the heel of your standing foot and explosively jump upwards, extending your hips and knee to propel your body off the ground.
7) Land softly on the same foot and immediately lower yourself back into a squat position upon landing, maintaining control and stability.
8) Repeat the movement for the desired repetitions and then switch sides.
This exercise targets your lower body strength, balance, and coordination while incorporating the instability challenge of the TRX suspension trainer.
1) Stand facing the TRX suspension trainer, holding both handles with an overhand grip. Adjust the straps to hang at mid-length.
2) Balance on one leg, lifting the opposite foot off the ground slightly in front of you. Keep your chest up and shoulders back for proper posture.
3) Lower your body by bending your knee and hip, allowing the suspended foot to extend forward as you descend. Aim to keep your raised foot off the ground throughout the movement.
4) Lower yourself as far as your flexibility allows, ideally reaching a seated position with your thigh parallel to the floor. Maintain a straight back and avoid rounding your shoulders.
5) Pause briefly at the bottom position, then push through your heel to return to the starting position, straightening your standing leg.
6) Maintain control throughout the exercise, engaging your core and focusing on stability. Repeat the movement for the desired number of repetitions on one leg before switching to the other.
The TRX pistol squat challenges balance, strength, and flexibility, particularly targeting your quadriceps, glutes, and core muscles.

1) Place your feet in the TRX straps, adjusting them so they are suspended a few inches off the ground. Begin in a plank position with your hands directly under your shoulders and your body forming a straight line from head to heels. This will be your starting position.
2) Engage your core muscles to maintain stability and ensure your spine is in a neutral position. Keep your head in line with your spine, and avoid letting your hips sag or rise.
3) In a controlled motion, bring your knees towards your chest, keeping your feet together and maintaining a tight core. Your hips will rise slightly as you tuck your knees in.
4) Pause for a moment, feeling the contraction in your abs and hip flexors.
5) Extend your legs back to the starting plank position, straightening your body once again.
6) Continue for the recommended number of repetitions, focusing on maintaining a steady and controlled movement.
7) Remember to breathe consistently and avoid any sudden or jerky movements.
This TRX Atomic Abs exercise effectively targets your core muscles, enhancing strength and stability.
Grab a dumbbell in each hand and adopt a standing position. Your arms should be fully extended, elbows close to your body with your palms facing away from you. This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arm still, curl the dumbbells towards your shoulders by contracting your biceps as you exhale. Tip: Keep your elbows close to your body throughout the movement. Only your forearms should move.
Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze your biceps.
Whilst at the top of the movement, you then rotate your wrist so that you have a pronated grip (palms down).
With this grip, descend the dumbbells along the same plane you brought them up on.
Once you return to the starting position, rotate your wrists once again until your palms are facing away from you.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Grab two dumbbells with a supinated grip (palms facing forward), elbows close to your torso and your arms at full extension. This will be your starting position.
As you exhale, curl the dumbbells up whilst dragging your elbows backwards until you can contract your biceps no more. Tip: Try to stop your shoulders from lifting.
Pause at this contracted position for a second before returning the dumbbells along the same plane to the starting point. Be sure to inhale as you perform this section of the movement.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variation: Barbell Curl.
Take hold of the pull up bars with a neutral grip (hands should be facing each other).
Both arms should be extended as you create a small curvature in your back and push your chest out. This will be your starting position.

Now, exhale and pull your torso up until your head is at least level with your hands. Concentrate on squeezing your shoulder blades back and down (through your lats) as you reach the fully contracted position.

After a brief pause at the contracted position, inhale as you slowly return your torso to the starting position. Track the same path down as you did up. You are at the starting position when your lats are fully stretched.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variation: Wide Grip Pull Ups.

Load up the recommended weight onto the machine and adjust the height of the seat so that the handles are at chest level. Grab one of the handles with either a neutral or pronated (palms facing down) grip. Use your other hand for support (being sure to keep away from any moving parts). This will be your starting position.
Exhale and pull the handle in towards your body by flexing at the elbow. Retract your shoulder blades and engage your back as you go.
Pause at this position, inhale and slowly return the handles to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variation: One-Arm Seated Row Machine (Neutral Grip).

Grab a medicine ball of the required weight and take a seat on the floor. Hold the medicine ball above your hips (flexing slightly at the elbow) and create an imaginary 'V' shape with your body by leaning back and lifting your feet off the floor. Tip: Your knees should be bent and if any pressure comes through your lower back, replace your feet back onto the floor. This is the starting position.
Twist your torso to the right hand side until the medicine ball is just off the floor. Exhale as you twist, making sure that your head follows your shoulders.
Reverse the movement towards the left side keeping your breathing constant and exhaling as the weight is lowered either side.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: V Sit With Plate Rotations or Seated Barbell Twists.

Load on the desired amount of weight and take a seat on the machine. Place one of your feet on the platform directly in front of you and adopt a stabilising stance with your other foot (out of the way of any moving equipment). This will be your starting position.
As you inhale, slowly flex at your knee until it is at 90 degrees. Be sure not to allow your knee to collapse in when performing this movement.
As you exhale, Press through your heel to push the foot plate away from you until you return to the starting position with your quads and glutes under tension.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Leg Press or Leg Press (Narrow Stance).
Load on the desired amount of weight and take a seat on the machine. Place your feet on the platform directly in front of you and adopt a wide stance. This will be your starting position.
As you inhale, slowly flex at your knees until they are at 90 degrees. Be sure not to allow your knees to collapse in when performing this movement.
As you exhale, Press through your heels to push the foot plate away from you, until you return to the starting position with your quads and glutes under tension.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Leg Press or Leg Press (Narrow Stance).
Firstly, adjust the lever where your ankle will be so that it matches your height. When you lay face down on the machine the lever should be a few inches below your calves.

Once you have set the levers, lay face down on the machine and grab the handles positioned on the inside of the machine. Your toes should be straight and pointed towards the floor. This will be your starting position.
While exhaling, curl one of your heels up towards your glutes. Once you are at the top of the contraction, hold for a second before lowering your leg towards the starting position.
Inhale as you do this and repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: Do not allow your hips to lift as you contract your hamstring as this will put undue stress on the lower back.
Variations: Lying Hamstring Curl or Standing Hamstring Curl Machine.

Place the recommended amount of weight on the machine and take a seat. Once you are seated, place you feet on the foot plate with your toes pointing together, with your heels apart. Next, adjust the height of the lever pad and place your lower thighs under it. Place your hands on top of the lever pad in order to prevent it from slipping.
Lift the lever slightly by pushing through the balls of your feet and release the safety bar. This will be your starting position.
Inhale as you slowly lower your heels towards the floor and continue until your calves are fully stretched.

As you exhale, press through the balls of your feet, contracting the calves until your ankles are as high as possible. Hold and squeeze your calves for a second before repeating the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Seated Calf Raise Machine or Seated Calf Raise Machine (Toes Out).

Place the recommended amount of weight on the machine and take a seat. Once you are seated, place the balls of your feet on the foot plate with your toes pointed outward to create a 'V' shape with your heels. Next, adjust the height of the lever pad and place your lower thighs under it. Place your hands on top of the lever pad in order to prevent it from slipping.
Lift the lever slightly by pushing through the balls of your feet and release the safety bar. This will be your starting position.
Inhale as you slowly lower your heels towards the floor and continue until your calves are fully stretched.

As you exhale, press through the balls of your feet, contracting the calves until your ankles are as high as possible. Hold and squeeze your calves for a second before repeating the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Seated Calf Raise Machine or Seated Calf Raise Machine (Toes In).

Place the recommended weight onto the machine and take a seat with your legs firmly secured in position. You should take hold of the handles and start with a 90 degree bend at your elbows. This is your starting position.
As you exhale, press the handles towards the floor until your elbows are fully extended.
At this position you should squeeze your triceps and pause for a second.
Then, as you inhale, reverse the movement until your elbows are flexed at 90 degrees and you have returned to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Cable Tricep Extension or Dumbbell Tricep Kickbacks.
Using a 45 degree leg press machine, take a seat and place one of your feet on the foot plate.

Gently press the foot plate away from you as you unhook the lock bars. You will take the weight at this point, ensure your knee is not locked and your joints remain slightly flexed. This will be your starting position.
As you inhale, slowly lower the platform until your upper and lower leg makes a 90-degree angle.
As you exhale, press through your heel to push the foot plate away from you, engaging your quadriceps and glutes in order to do so.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and ensure to lock the safety pins properly once you are done.
Caution: Always securely lock the safety bars once you have finished.
Variations: 45 Degree Leg Press (Wide Stance), 45 Degree Leg Press (Shoulder Width Stance) or 45 Degree Leg Press (Narrow Stance)
Using a 45 degree leg press machine, take a seat and place your feet on the foot plate to create a wide stance.

Gently press the foot plate away from you as you unhook the lock bars. You will take the weight at this point, ensure your knees are not locked and your joints remain slightly flexed. This will be your starting position.
As you inhale, slowly lower the platform until your upper and lower legs make a 90-degree angle.
As you exhale, press through your heels to push the foot plate away from you, engaging your quadriceps and glutes in order to do so.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and ensure to lock the safety pins properly once you are done.
Caution: Always securely lock the safety bars once you have finished.
Variations: 45 Degree Leg Press (Shoulder Width Stance) or Barbell Side Lunges.
Using a 45 degree leg press machine, take a seat and place your feet together to create a narrow stance.

Gently press the foot plate away from you as you unhook the lock bars. You will take the weight at this point, ensure your knees are not locked and your joints remain slightly flexed. This will be your starting position.
As you inhale, slowly lower the platform until your upper and lower legs make a 90-degree angle.
As you exhale, press through your heels to push the foot plate away from you, engaging your quadriceps and glutes in order to do so.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and ensure to lock the safety pins properly once you are done.
Caution: Always securely lock the safety bars once you have finished.
Variations: Hack Squats (Narrow Stance) or TRX Single Leg Squats.

name_en: 45 Degree Leg Press Shoulder Width Stance
instructions_en: Using a 45 degree leg press machine, take a seat and place your feet at a shoulder width stance.

Gently press the foot plate away from you as you unhook the lock bars. You will take the weight at this point, ensure your knees are not locked and your joints remain slightly flexed. This will be your starting position.
As you inhale, slowly lower the platform until your upper and lower legs make a 90-degree angle.
As you exhale, press through your heels to push the foot plate away from you, engaging your quadriceps and glutes in order to do so.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and ensure to lock the safety pins properly once you are done.
Caution: Always securely lock the safety bars once you have finished.
Variations: Barbell Squats or Dumbbell Squats.
To begin, seat yourself on the bike and adjust the seat to your height.
Start pedalling and adjust the resistance to the level you want.
Remember: When you are coming to the end of your cycle, DO NOT stop pedalling without applying the brake or coming down to a gentle stop. Otherwise there is a danger of injury to your joints.

Set the weight on the machine to the recommended resistance and set the height of the seat so that your chest support is on your sternum. You should adopt an overhand grip with one of your hands. Your other hand should be used for support. This will be your starting position.
Pull the handle towards your torso, ensuring your elbow is at shoulder height. Exhale as you retract your elbow until your shoulder blades can retract no more.
Pause at this point for a second and then slowly return the handle to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

Set the weight on the machine to the recommended resistance and set the height of the seat so that your chest support is on your sternum. You should adopt an overhand grip. This will be your starting position.
Pull the handles towards your torso, ensuring your elbows at shoulder height. Exhale as you retract your elbows until your shoulder blades can retract no more.
Pause at this point for a second and then slowly return the handles to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

Set the weight on the machine to the recommended resistance and set the height on the seat so that the chest support is on your sternum. You should adopt a neutral grip with one of your hands, the other hand should be used for stability. This will be your starting position.
Pull the handle towards your torso, exhale and retract your shoulder blades as you flex your elbow.
Pause at this point for a second and then slowly return the handle to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

Set the weight on the machine to the recommended resistance and set the height on the seat so that the chest support is on your sternum. You should adopt a neutral grip. This will be your starting position.
Pull the handle towards your torso, exhale and retract your shoulder blades as you flex your elbow (keeping your elbow parallel to the floor as you go).
Pause at this point for a second and then slowly return the handle back along the same path to its starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Stand with a narrow or shoulder width stance with a weight plate in your right hand. Have your elbow fully extended with the plate by your side. This will be your starting position.

Whilst keeping your back straight and your head up, bend at the waist to the right as far as possible. Be sure to inhale as you bend to the side. Hold at the bottom of the movement for a second and come back up to the starting position as you exhale.
Tip: The rest of your body must remain stationary.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and do the same for the left hand side.
Caution: Exercise caution if you have lower back problems. Stop immediately
Variations: Dumbbell Side Bends or Barbell Side Bends
Whilst in a standing position, grab two dumbbells and keep your arms by your sides. This will be your starting position.

Take a step forward (slightly larger than a normal step) and as you land, drop your body down so that you create two right angles at your knees. Inhale as you do so whilst maintaining a straight torso.
Make sure that you keep your front knee over your heel and not over your toes. Your front shin should now be perpendicular to the floor.
As you exhale, push off the floor with your rear foot to return your body into a standing position.
Alternate legs as you go (as you walk forwards) and repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Bodyweight Walking Lunges or Barbell Walking Lunges.
To begin, stand straight up grabbing a wrist roller using a pronated grip (palms facing down).  Your feet should be shoulder width apart.

Slowly lift both arms until they are fully extended and parallel to the floor in front of you.  Note: Make sure the rope is not wrapped around the roller.  Your entire body should be stationary except for the forearms.  This is the starting position.
Rotate one wrist at a time in an upward motion to bring the weight up to the bar by rolling the rope around the roller.
Once the weight has reached the bar, slowly begin to lower the weight back down by rotating the wrist in a downward motion until the weight reaches the starting position.
Repeat for the prescribed amount of repetitions in your program.

You will need two cable handle attachments and a flat bench, as well as chains, for this exercise. Clip the middle of the chains to the handles, and position yourself on the flat bench. Your elbows should be pointing straight up.
Begin by extending through the elbow, keeping your upper arm still, with your wrists pronated.
Pause at the lockout, and reverse the motion to return to the starting position.
Suspend your straps so the handles are 18-30 inches from the floor.
Facing away from the setup, place your rear foot into the handle behind you. Keep your head looking forward and your chest up, with your knee slightly bent. This will be your starting position.
Descend by flexing the knee and hips, lowering yourself to the ground. Keep your weight on the heel of your foot and maintain your posture throughout the exercise.
At the bottom of the movement, reverse the motion, extending through the hip and knee to return to the starting position.

Suspend your straps at around chest height. Take a handle in each hand and lean back. Keep your body erect and your head and chest up. Your arms should be fully extended. This will be your starting position.
Begin by flexing the elbow to initiate the movement. Protract your shoulder blades as you do so.
At the completion of the motion pause, and then return to the starting position.
Secure a set of suspension straps with the handles hanging about a foot off of the ground. Move yourself into a pushup plank position facing away from the rack.
Place your feet into the handles. You should maintain a straight posture, not allowing the hips to sag. This will be your starting position.
Begin the movement by flexing the knees and hips, drawing the knees to your torso. As you do so, anteriorly tilt your pelvis, allowing your spine to flex.
At the top of the controlled motion, return to the starting position.
Anchor your suspension straps securely to the top of a rack or other object.

Leaning into the straps, take a handle in each hand and move into a push-up plank position. You should be as close to parallel to the ground as you can manage with your arms fully extended, maintaining good posture.
